---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

## Why Compositional Robustness Does Not Obviate the Probabilistic Model {#sec-diffexp-why-model}

The preceding section established a powerful result: CLR-based compositional
inference is exactly invariant to capture probability misspecification. Combined
with the well-known fact that library-size normalization produces unbiased
composition estimates, a natural question arises:

> *If gene compositions are so robust that even simple normalization recovers
> them correctly, why do we need a probabilistic model at all? Is the entire
> framework just an elaborate way to compute $u_g / u_T$?*

This section shows that the robustness of compositional *point estimates* is a
mathematical inevitability---a consequence of compositions being ratios of
expected counts, which are tightly constrained by the data regardless of the
model. We then demonstrate that point estimates without calibrated uncertainty
are insufficient for differential expression, and that the gap between naive
normalization and the Dirichlet-Multinomial model is precisely the
*overdispersion structure* that makes single-cell biology interesting.

### Compositions are ratios of expected counts {#sec-diffexp-why-model-means}

We first establish a unified principle that explains why compositions are
robust across all model variants considered in this work.

#### Shared-$p$ model

Under the standard Dirichlet-Multinomial factorization
(@eq-hier-factorization-recap), the compositional distribution is
$\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{r} \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r})$.
The expected composition for gene $g$ is

$$
\langle \rho_g \mid \underline{r} \rangle = \frac{r_g}{r_T},
$${#eq-why-model-comp-shared}

where $r_T = \sum_{g'} r_{g'}$. The expected count per gene under the negative
binomial model is $\mu_g = r_g(1 - \hat{p})/\hat{p}$, and the total expected
count is $\mu_T = r_T(1 - \hat{p})/\hat{p}$. Since the shared factor $(1 -
\hat{p})/\hat{p}$ cancels in the ratio,

$$
\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_T} = \frac{r_g(1-\hat{p})/\hat{p}}{r_T(1-\hat{p})/\hat{p}}
= \frac{r_g}{r_T} = \langle \rho_g \mid \underline{r} \rangle.
$${#eq-why-model-mean-ratio-shared}

The expected composition is identically the ratio of expected counts.

#### Gene-specific $p_g$ model

Under the hierarchical model developed in @sec-hierarchical-p, compositions are
generated via the Gamma-based procedure (@eq-hier-summary-composition):

$$
\gamma_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, 1), \qquad
\rho_g = \frac{
    \gamma_g \cdot (1-p_g)/p_g
}{
    \sum_{j} \gamma_j \cdot (1-p_j)/p_j
}.
$${#eq-why-model-gamma-comp}

The expected value of $\gamma_g \cdot (1 - p_g)/p_g$ is

$$
\langle \gamma_g \rangle \cdot \frac{1-p_g}{p_g} = r_g \cdot \frac{1-p_g}{p_g}
= \mu_g,
$${#eq-why-model-gamma-mean}

which is exactly the expected count for gene $g$ under $\text{NB}(r_g, p_g)$.
By a standard result on ratios of independent random variables with shared
normalizer, the expected composition is

$$
\langle \rho_g \rangle \approx \frac{\mu_g}{\mu_T},
$${#eq-why-model-mean-ratio-hier}

where the approximation becomes exact in the limit $r_g \to \infty$ (the
Dirichlet concentration limit). For the parameter ranges encountered in
single-cell data, the approximation is excellent.

#### The mean calibration constraint

In both model variants, the expected composition is determined by the ratio
$\mu_g / \mu_T$. The per-gene expected count $\mu_g$ is pinned by the data
through the sample mean:

$$
\bar{u}_g = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C u_{gc} \xrightarrow{C \to \infty} \mu_g.
$${#eq-why-model-sample-mean}

With $C$ cells, the relative precision of $\bar{u}_g$ as an estimator of
$\mu_g$ is of order $1/\sqrt{C}$. For typical single-cell datasets with
$C \sim 10^3$--$10^5$ cells per condition, this precision is $0.3$--$3\%$. The
sample mean is the most tightly constrained quantity in any count model---it is
a sufficient statistic for the Poisson family, and the NB likelihood
inherits this property for the mean direction.

This has a profound consequence: **any model that fits the data will match the
per-gene means, and therefore the composition point estimates, regardless of
its internal parameterization.** The model may decompose $\mu_g$ into $r_g$ and
$\hat{p}$ (shared-$p$), or into $r_g$ and $p_g$ (gene-specific), or into
entirely different parameter combinations---but the product must always
reproduce $\mu_g$, and the composition $\mu_g / \mu_T$ is invariant to the
decomposition.

This explains a striking empirical observation from synthetic validation
studies: even when a model is severely misspecified (e.g., fitting a
shared-parameter model to data generated with per-cell heterogeneity), the
compositional recovery remains excellent despite catastrophic failure in
absolute parameter recovery. The absolute parameters $r_g$ and $p$ can be
badly wrong, but $r_g(1-p)/p$ must still match $\mu_g$, so the ratios
$\mu_g / \mu_T$ are preserved.

### Simple normalization recovers the same point estimates {#sec-diffexp-why-model-normalization}

The standard library-size normalization computes

$$
\hat{\rho}_g^{\text{norm}} = \frac{u_g}{u_T},
$${#eq-why-model-naive-norm}

where $u_T = \sum_{g'} u_{g'}$ is the total UMI count for the cell. This
estimator has the property

$$
\left\langle \hat{\rho}_g^{\text{norm}} \mid u_T \right\rangle
= \frac{\langle u_g \mid u_T \rangle}{u_T},
$${#eq-why-model-naive-cond-mean}

and under the multinomial distribution conditional on $u_T$,

$$
\langle u_g \mid u_T \rangle = u_T \cdot \rho_g,
$${#eq-why-model-multinomial-mean}

giving $\left\langle \hat{\rho}_g^{\text{norm}} \mid u_T \right\rangle = \rho_g$. The
estimator is conditionally unbiased. Averaging over cells, the sample-level
composition estimate

$$
\hat{\rho}_g = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^C \frac{u_{gc}}{u_{T,c}}
$${#eq-why-model-sample-comp}

converges to the true composition $\rho_g$ as $C \to \infty$. Under variable
capture, the multinomial conditioning on $u_T$ automatically integrates out
$\nu_c$, so $\hat{\rho}_g^{\text{norm}}$ is robust to variable capture for the
same structural reason as the model-based estimate.

For *point estimation* of compositions, simple normalization and the full
probabilistic model produce the same answer. This equality is exact: it follows
from the mean-calibration constraint.

### The gap: overdispersion and the variance of compositions {#sec-diffexp-why-model-gap}

If the point estimates are the same, what does the probabilistic model provide?
The answer is: **calibrated uncertainty**. And the critical distinction is
between the *multinomial* model (which simple normalization implicitly assumes)
and the *Dirichlet-Multinomial* model (which scribe uses).

#### The multinomial model of compositions

Library-size normalization, followed by a standard test (e.g., a $t$-test or
Wilcoxon test on $u_g / u_T$ across cells), implicitly treats the count vector
$\underline{u}$ as drawn from a Multinomial distribution conditional on $u_T$:

$$
\underline{u} \mid u_T \sim \text{Multinomial}(u_T, \underline{\rho}).
$${#eq-why-model-multinomial}

Under this model, the variance of the per-cell composition estimate for gene
$g$ is

$$
\text{Var}_{\text{Mult}}(\hat{\rho}_g) = \frac{\rho_g(1-\rho_g)}{u_T}.
$${#eq-why-model-multinomial-var}

This is pure *sampling noise*: if the cell had more UMIs, the estimate would
be more precise. The multinomial treats all cells as having the same underlying
composition $\underline{\rho}$, with variation arising solely from the discrete
sampling of molecules.

#### The Dirichlet-Multinomial model of compositions

The scribe model, by contrast, recognizes that the composition itself is a
random variable drawn from a Dirichlet distribution:

$$
\underline{\rho} \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r}), \qquad
\underline{u} \mid u_T, \underline{\rho} \sim
\text{Multinomial}(u_T, \underline{\rho}).
$${#eq-why-model-dm}

Marginalizing over $\underline{\rho}$, the count vector follows the
Dirichlet-Multinomial distribution $\text{DM}(\underline{u} \mid u_T,
\underline{r})$, and the marginal variance of the per-cell composition becomes

$$
\text{Var}_{\text{DM}}(\hat{\rho}_g) =
\frac{\rho_g(1-\rho_g)}{u_T}
\cdot \frac{u_T + r_T}{1 + r_T}.
$${#eq-why-model-dm-var}

The variance is inflated relative to the multinomial by the factor

$$
\text{overdispersion factor} = \frac{u_T + r_T}{1 + r_T}.
$${#eq-why-model-overdispersion-factor}

This factor captures the *biological cell-to-cell heterogeneity* in
transcriptional programs that is the very reason single-cell experiments are
performed. It reflects the fact that different cells of the same type do not
have identical transcriptional compositions---they vary according to the
Dirichlet distribution governed by $\underline{r}$.

#### Magnitude of the overdispersion gap

The overdispersion factor depends on the concentration parameter $r_T$ relative
to the library size $u_T$. To build intuition, consider the typical parameter
ranges in single-cell RNA-seq:

- **Library size**: $u_T \sim 2{,}000$--$20{,}000$ UMIs per cell.
- **Concentration**: $r_T = \sum_g r_g$ is typically in the range
  $500$--$5{,}000$, depending on the gene set and cell type.

For these values, the overdispersion factor @eq-why-model-overdispersion-factor
ranges from approximately $2\times$ (when $r_T$ is comparable to $u_T$) to
$5$--$10\times$ (when $r_T$ is small relative to $u_T$). The DM-model composition
variance can be **an order of magnitude larger** than the multinomial-model
variance.

In the limit $r_T \to \infty$ (no biological overdispersion), the factor
converges to 1 and the DM reduces to the multinomial. In the limit $r_T \to 0$
(extreme overdispersion), it diverges, reflecting maximally heterogeneous
compositions. Single-cell data live in the intermediate regime where the
correction is substantial but finite---precisely the regime where getting it
right matters.

### Consequences for differential expression {#sec-diffexp-why-model-de}

The overdispersion gap has immediate and catastrophic consequences for
differential expression analysis when the wrong model is used.

#### False positive inflation under the multinomial

Consider a gene $g$ that is *not* differentially expressed between conditions A
and B ($\rho_g^A = \rho_g^B$). A two-sample test on the per-cell normalized
values $u_{gc}/u_{T,c}$ computes a test statistic based on the estimated
variance. Under the multinomial model, this variance is
@eq-why-model-multinomial-var. Under the DM model, the true variance is
@eq-why-model-dm-var, which is larger by the overdispersion factor.

A test that uses the multinomial variance---as any test based on simple
normalization implicitly does---will produce a test statistic that is inflated
by the square root of the overdispersion factor. For a $5\times$ overdispersion
factor, this translates to a $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.2\times$ inflation in the
$z$-score. In genome-wide testing with $D \sim 20{,}000$ genes, this inflation
turns a controlled false discovery rate into a flood of false positives.

Quantitatively, suppose the true DM effect is zero and the overdispersion factor
is $\omega$. A test based on the multinomial variance computes

$$
z_{\text{Mult}} = \frac{\bar{\rho}_g^A - \bar{\rho}_g^B}{
    \sqrt{\text{Var}_{\text{Mult}}^A / C_A +
          \text{Var}_{\text{Mult}}^B / C_B}
} = \sqrt{\omega} \; z_{\text{DM}}.
$${#eq-why-model-z-inflation}

Under the null, $z_{\text{DM}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$, but $z_{\text{Mult}}
\sim \mathcal{N}(0, \omega)$. The tail probability at threshold $z^*$ is

$$
P(|z_{\text{Mult}}| > z^*) = 2\,\Phi\!\left(-z^*/\sqrt{\omega}\right),
$${#eq-why-model-fpr}

which can be vastly larger than the nominal $2\Phi(-z^*)$. For $\omega = 5$ and
$z^* = 3$ (a nominal two-sided $p$-value of $0.003$), the actual false positive
rate is $2\Phi(-3/\sqrt{5}) = 2\Phi(-1.34) \approx 18\%$---a $60\times$
inflation.

#### Multiple testing corrections cannot rescue anti-conservative p-values {#sec-diffexp-why-model-mtc}

A natural response to the false positive inflation above is to invoke
*multiple testing corrections*---standard machinery in genome-wide analyses
that adjusts significance thresholds to account for the large number of
simultaneous tests. The two most widely used corrections are:

- **Bonferroni correction**: reject the null for gene $g$ if $p_g <
  \alpha / D$, where $D$ is the number of genes tested and $\alpha$ is the
  family-wise error rate (FWER). This guarantees that the probability of *any*
  false rejection is at most $\alpha$.

- **Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) procedure**: rank the p-values
  $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(D)}$, find the largest index
  $k$ such that $p_{(k)} \leq k \cdot q / D$, and reject all genes with rank
  $\leq k$. This controls the false discovery rate (FDR)---the expected
  fraction of rejections that are false---at level $q$.

Both methods are technically correct: they provide exactly the guarantees they
promise, *provided that the input p-values are valid under the null*. Validity
means that for a truly non-DE gene, the p-value is uniformly distributed on
$[0, 1]$ (or at least super-uniform, i.e., $P(p_g \leq t) \leq t$ for all
$t$). The overdispersion problem violates this assumption at its root: the
inflated $z$-scores produce p-values that are *anti-conservative*---too small
under the null. No downstream correction can repair an input that is already
miscalibrated.

To see this concretely, consider the Bonferroni correction with $D = 20{,}000$
genes and FWER $\alpha = 0.05$. The per-gene threshold is $\alpha / D = 2.5
\times 10^{-6}$, corresponding to a z-score cutoff of

$$
z^* = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2D}\right) \approx 4.71.
$${#eq-why-model-bonf-z}

Without overdispersion, a null gene exceeds this threshold with probability
$2.5 \times 10^{-6}$. Across $D$ null genes, the expected number of false
positives is $D \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.05$, and the FWER guarantee
holds.

With overdispersion factor $\omega = 5$, the test computes
$z_{\text{Mult}} = \sqrt{5}\, z_{\text{DM}}$, so a null gene exceeds the
Bonferroni threshold when

$$
|z_{\text{DM}}| > z^* / \sqrt{\omega} = 4.71 / \sqrt{5} \approx 2.11.
$${#eq-why-model-bonf-effective}

The probability of this event is $2\Phi(-2.11) \approx 0.035$---roughly
$3.5\%$ per gene, which is $14{,}000$ times larger than the $2.5 \times
10^{-6}$ that Bonferroni assumed. Across $20{,}000$ null genes, the expected
number of false positives is

$$
20{,}000 \times 0.035 = 700 \;\text{false positives},
$${#eq-why-model-bonf-fp}

instead of the $0.05$ that the FWER guarantee promised. Bonferroni ``corrected''
nothing---it applied a perfectly valid correction to perfectly invalid inputs.

The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure fares no better. BH is deliberately *less
conservative* than Bonferroni (rejecting more genes to gain power), so it
admits even more false positives than the Bonferroni count above. The FDR
guarantee of ``at most $q$ fraction of discoveries are false'' is void because
BH assumes $P(p_g \leq t \mid \text{null}) \leq t$, which fails catastrophically
when the p-values are anti-conservative.

The lesson is general: **multiple testing corrections address the problem of
testing many genes simultaneously; they do not and cannot address the problem
of each individual test being miscalibrated.** The overdispersion error enters
at the per-gene level---in the variance estimate used to compute the test
statistic. No amount of threshold adjustment at the genome-wide level can
compensate for a systematic $\sqrt{\omega}$-fold inflation that occurs before
the p-value is ever computed. It is, in the analogy of security, putting an
excellent lock on a door with no walls.

#### What the scribe posterior provides

The scribe model computes the full posterior over $\underline{r}$ for each
condition. Drawing Dirichlet samples $\underline{\rho}^{(s)} \sim
\text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r}^{(s)})$ (or Gamma-ratio samples under the
gene-specific $p_g$ model, @sec-hier-gamma-sampling), transforming to CLR, and
computing paired differences $\Delta_g^{(s)} = z_{\text{CLR},g}^{A,(s)} -
z_{\text{CLR},g}^{B,(s)}$, the empirical pipeline automatically propagates the
correct DM variance through every downstream quantity:

- **lfsr**: The local false sign rate is computed by counting the fraction of
  posterior samples with the minority sign, directly reflecting the DM-model
  uncertainty.
- **PEFP**: The genome-wide error control (@sec-diffexp-pefp) operates on these
  calibrated lfsr values.
- **Credible intervals**: The 68\% or 95\% intervals on $\Delta_g$ span the
  correct DM-model width, not the artificially narrow multinomial width.

No distributional assumption is needed---the Monte Carlo procedure inherits
the overdispersion directly from the Dirichlet (or Gamma-ratio) sampling step.

### Summary: what each approach provides {#sec-diffexp-why-model-summary}

The following table distills the comparison between simple normalization and
the full probabilistic model:

| Quantity | Simple normalization | Probabilistic model (scribe) |
|----------|---------------------|------|
| Composition point estimates | Correct (via $u_g/u_T$) | Correct (via posterior mean) |
| Robustness to variable capture | Robust | Robust |
| Variance model | Multinomial ($\propto 1/u_T$) | Dirichlet-Multinomial (overdispersed) |
| Overdispersion captured | No | Yes ($r_T$-dependent inflation) |
| Per-gene test calibration | Anti-conservative ($\sqrt{\omega}$ z-inflation) | Calibrated (posterior sampling) |
| MTC (Bonferroni/BH) effectiveness | Void (input p-values invalid) | N/A (Bayesian PEFP instead) |
| Error control | Nominal only; actual FP $\sim 700$ at FWER $0.05$ | PEFP at specified level |
| Full posterior over compositions | No | Yes |
| Gene-gene correlations | No | Yes (via low-rank guide) |
| Denoising (count-scale recovery) | No | Yes (Bayesian posterior means) |
| Capture efficiency estimation | No | Yes |
| Gene-specific $p_g$ detection | No | Yes (hierarchical model, $\sigma_p$ diagnostic) |

The probabilistic model's contribution is not better composition point
estimates---both approaches get these right. Its contribution is calibrated
uncertainty quantification, overdispersion-aware differential expression,
principled error control, and the full suite of downstream inferences
(denoising, gene-set analysis, capture estimation) that require a generative
model of the measurement process.

Robust compositions are a *consequence* of the framework: the core quantity of
interest (gene compositions) is well-identified and stable across model
variants, providing a solid foundation on which to build reliable differential
expression analysis. The probabilistic model does not improve the first decimal
place of the composition estimate; it quantifies which digits are supported by
the data.
